text: So as all of you know, there are many ways to surgically correct a parasophageal hernia. In this video, I'm going to describe my preferred method, which is a robotic-assisted repair with an anterior fundoplication, and I'll try to describe my logic behind my decision as the video goes along.
So the patient's an elderly woman with a classic type III parasophageal hernia. with severe symptoms.

The coronal and sagittal imaging cuts show the fundus and gastroesophageal (GE) junction positioned above the diaphragm, indicating the anatomical displacement associated with the parasophageal hernia.

The surgeon demonstrates the port setup for the robotic-assisted hernia repair, utilizing all four robotic arms. A 30-degree down camera is positioned just to the left of the umbilicus, avoiding interference with the falciform ligament.

The surgeon uses an 8mm interseal assistant port on the lateral edge while the patient is positioned in steep reverse Trendelenburg. A Nathanson liver retractor is placed in the subxiphoid space to retract the liver anteriorly.
The video begins with a view of the surgical port setup, showing the introduction of the camera into the abdominal cavity for the hernia repair procedure.

The surgical view is captured using a thirty-degree down camera positioned to the left of the umbilicus, providing a clear view of the surgical field while avoiding obstruction by the falciform ligament.
And this patient had had some prior surgery. I think she had a colon resection.
The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device on the highest setting to dissect through tissue, with an option to use a spatula for this step.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to reduce the visible parasophageal hernia, manipulating the herniated tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon gently reduces the parasophageal hernia without tearing the serosa of the stomach, using bipolar cautery to dissect through the gastrohepatic omentum.

The surgeon examines the area for a replaced left hepatic artery, noting the absence of significant branches. Using bipolar cautery, the surgeon proceeds to cauterize the small tissue remnants in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the right crus of the diaphragm, aiming to access the space between the diaphragm muscle and the hernia sac. A visible line guides the entry into this space.

And this might be a good time to talk about just the philosophy of reducing the hernia sac. and trying to preserve that plane between the hernia sac and the mediastinum and the cruise of the diaphragm. I don't go crazy about preserving that plane if it's hard to do. If it's easy to do and the plane separates easily, I'll go for it.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the hernia sac and the right crus of the diaphragm, revealing a thin separation of tissue layers. This dissection is crucial for reducing the hernia sac in large parasophageal hernias, such as type four, to facilitate the repositioning of the stomach.
The surgeon visually identifies the junction where the right and left crura of the diaphragm converge posteriorly, with the left and right crura clearly visible.

The surgeon begins dissecting the left crus of the diaphragm using bipolar cautery. The table-side assistant pulls the stomach down and to the left, while the surgeon's fourth arm pulls everything to the right, revealing the anatomical landmark.

The surgeon dissects the space between the gastroesophageal junction and the left crus of the diaphragm, focusing on the angle of His area. The tissue appears dense, indicating the hernia has likely been present for a long time, making the dissection challenging.

In the bottom right corner of the screen, the table-side assistant pulls the stomach towards the patient's left. The surgeon uses a long instrument with a tip-up configuration to maneuver underneath the esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction, preparing to place a Penrose drain around the esophagus.
The surgeon uses an instrument as a retractor at the gastroesophageal junction, applying extra traction to facilitate the transition of the case.

The surgeon places a Penrose drain around the esophagus at the angle of His, cutting a small slit in the drain to use it as a gentle retractor. The assistant holds the drain up, allowing the surgeon to gain length on the esophagus. The robotic approach provides enhanced visualization, facilitating access into the posterior mediastinum.
The surgeon inadvertently punctures into the right pleural space during dissection, with visible entry into the pleural cavity.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer extend to divide the connective tissue attachments between the esophagus and the mediastinal structures, carefully separating these tissues.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer extend to carefully dissect through connective tissue around the esophagus, maintaining a margin to avoid the posterior vagus nerve, which is visible and incorporated in the Penrose drain. The goal is to achieve three to four centimeters of esophageal length below the diaphragm without tension.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to elevate the Penrose drain while the assistant pulls down on the gastric fat pad. The esophagus is encircled and freed from its connective tissue attachments within the chest, achieving additional length below the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm and assistance to expose the surgical field with a thirty-degree down camera angle, dividing the connective tissue attachments around the esophagus. The pleura on the right side is likely breached during this process.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers closer to the esophagus to avoid entering the left pleural space, noting the anatomical challenges posed by the liver pushing against the right diaphragm and the diaphragm protruding on the left side.

The surgeon divides omental fat adhered to the underside of the diaphragm near the left crus, cleaning the area to prepare for mesh placement. The confluence of the crura is visible posteriorly as the surgeon clears the tissue to facilitate stitching.

The surgeon addresses additional connective tissue attachments between the esophagus and the mediastinum, focusing on achieving sufficient esophageal length without tension. The esophagus appears to have good length now, facilitating the hernia repair process.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect the plane between the gastric fat pad and the stomach, excising the fat pad to clearly visualize the anatomy, particularly the angle of His.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the stomach wall towards the angle of His, being cautious not to compromise the anterior vagus nerve, which is located on the left side of the esophagus and angles down anteriorly. The gastric fat pad has been removed, providing a clear view of the angle of His situated below the diaphragm without tension.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the esophagus, gaining additional length while working in the mediastinum. The descending aorta is visible, highlighting the enhanced visualization and dexterity provided by the robotic system.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the esophagus, leaving a margin to avoid compromising the vagus nerve. The fourth robotic arm is used to expose the area, revealing the vagus nerve below. The dissection is performed above and lateral to the vagus nerve to ensure its protection, maintaining a safe plane.

The surgeon manipulates the esophagus to gain additional length, rotating the view to examine it from underneath. A small amount of mental fat obstructs the view, while the descending aorta is visible below. The surgeon assesses the gastroesophageal junction for increased length.

The surgeon identifies the angle of His, noting connective tissue causing puckering at this anatomical landmark. The vagus nerve is visible curling up to the left, and the surgeon carefully avoids it while gaining additional length on the left lateral side of the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the Penrose drain with the fourth arm while the assistant holds the omentum away. The crura are well exposed, and the surgeon begins placing simple stitches using a zero Ethibond suture, preserving the peritoneal cell layer around the crura for stitch integrity. The stitches are tied using a TK or tie knot knot pusher, ensuring a secure knot.

The surgeon uses the Vessel Sealer Extend as a needle driver, demonstrating its dual function for suturing and cutting. The suture is fed to the table-side assistant, who uses a TK knot pusher to secure the knot without tension, aided by the surgeon's fourth arm holding the suture steady.

The surgeon begins reinforcing the hernia repair with bioabsorbable mesh, which will integrate into scar tissue over the next few months. The mesh is applied without pledges to avoid creating a mass that could complicate future interventions.

The surgeon preserves the peritoneal layer over the crura while feeding the second stitch to the table side assistant, using the fourth arm to hold up the Penrose drain. The third posterior stitch is placed without using a bougie, creating a snug closure around the esophagus with a partial wrap.
The surgeon focuses on placing posterior stitches to prevent hernia recurrence, emphasizing their importance. The mesh reinforcement is prepared for placement to support these stitches.

The surgeon uses the Vessel Sealer Extend to retract the omental fat and places an anterior stitch, achieving a snug closure around the esophagus with visible three to four centimeters of length without tension.

The surgeon uses suction to remove fluid, revealing the diaphragm where mesh will be placed. Omental fat is visible and being freed from attachments to ensure the mesh lies flush against the diaphragm.

The surgeon prepares to place the Acell mesh, made from pig bladder, in a U-shaped fashion around the posterior crura. The mesh is bioabsorbable and will eventually become scar tissue. The surgeon tacks the mesh to the underside of the diaphragm using three or four tacking stitches with 2-0 Ethibond sutures, while the table side assistant uses a TK knot pusher to secure the knots.
The surgeon places a posterior anchoring stitch directly on the crura and ties it down, securing the mesh in place.

The mesh is visibly annealed to the underside of the diaphragm, positioned securely. The surgeon prepares to start the fundoplication, with the angle of His and gastroesophageal junction clearly visible, showing three to four centimeters of esophageal length.

The surgeon pulls the stomach over to ensure mobility and prepares to fire the first stitch at the angle of His, located between the stomach and esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction. The stitch incorporates a portion of the mesh attached to the diaphragm, as part of a partial anterior fundoplication and gastropexy procedure.

These are two O-ethabons, by the way. In my experience, the three hundred sixty degree wrap can create a bunch of problems. even if you do it over a bougie, it can cause gas bloating, which is very uncomfortable to patients. In some cases, or I'd say in many cases, the gas bloating is even more bothersome than the reflux. And I think the patients with the big parasophageal hernias, their main problems are due to the fact that the stomach is herniated up in the chest and it's And just by reducing the stomach back into the abdomen with three or four centimeters of intra-abdominal length on the distal esophagus, you're going to correct most of their symptoms.

The surgeon performs a gastropexy and anterior fundoplication by anchoring the stomach below the diaphragm, using stitches to secure the diaphragm and stomach together. The procedure avoids taking down the short gastric vessels, which can prevent ischemia to the stomach fundus and spleen.
The surgeon places an additional stitch to ensure the fundoplication lays flat, addressing decreased motility in patients with parasophageal hernias.

The surgeon completes a partial anterior fundoplication, retrieving omental fat and hernia sac contents through a small bag. An esophagoscope is inserted to inspect the repair internally, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction is positioned below the diaphragm. The stomach is suctioned, concluding the procedure.
